title: A Conversation About Education
summary: A teacher and a parent meet to discuss the student's progress, learning style, and the best ways to support his development both at school and at home.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Good afternoon, Mr. Harris. Thank you for coming in. Please have a seat.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: Good afternoon. Thank you for arranging this meeting. I've been looking forward to hearing how Tom is getting on.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Overall, Tom has made some real progress this term, especially in written tasks. His essays have become much more structured, and he clearly enjoys literature.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: That's great to hear. He does love reading at home, so that makes sense. What about maths? He's mentioned finding it quite difficult lately.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Yes, I've noticed that too. He struggles a little with abstract concepts, but when we apply maths to real-world problems, he engages much more confidently. I think the issue is more about confidence than ability.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: That's interesting. Is there anything specific I can do at home to help him build that confidence?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Absolutely. I'd suggest practising with everyday situations — budgeting pocket money, measuring ingredients when cooking, or calculating distances on a map. Seeing maths in a practical context often helps students like Tom make sense of the theory.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: That's a really helpful suggestion. I'll try to include him more in those kinds of tasks. On another note, his teacher last year mentioned he had trouble focusing during longer lessons. Has that been an issue this year?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: It has come up occasionally. Tom does tend to lose focus after about thirty minutes if the activity doesn't change. I've been trying to vary the tasks more frequently in class, which seems to help. Short, focused tasks work better for him than long, single activities.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: Good to know. We've been limiting screen time at home, which I think has helped a little. He's sleeping better, and I've noticed he seems more alert in the mornings.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Sleep makes a significant difference for children at this age. It's great that you've worked on that. I'd also encourage Tom to read for at least twenty minutes before bed rather than using a screen, if that's possible.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: We've actually already started doing that, and he's taken to it well. He's currently reading a book about space, which he's absolutely fascinated by.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Space is wonderful for engaging curious young minds. If he's interested, I can recommend some science books at his reading level that might connect his love of reading with science as a subject.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: That would be brilliant, thank you. Is there anything else you'd like to flag before we finish?

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: Just one thing — Tom sometimes holds back from speaking up in group discussions, even when I know he has good ideas. Encouraging him to share his opinions at home — during meals, for example — might help him feel more comfortable doing so in class.

body.speaker: Parent
body.text: I'll definitely work on that. We sometimes rush through dinner, but I can see how having proper discussions could be really valuable. Thank you so much for your time and insights.

body.speaker: Teacher
body.text: It's my pleasure. Tom is a thoughtful and creative student, and with your support at home, I'm confident he'll continue to grow. Let's stay in touch.

annotations.meaningVi: tiến bộ như thế nào, đang làm thế nào
annotations.exampleEn: How is your daughter getting on at her new school?
Young learners often find abstract concepts harder to grasp.
Grammar is easier to learn in a practical context.
I couldn't make sense of the instructions.

annotations.meaningVi: gặp khó khăn khi tập trung
annotations.exampleEn: He often has trouble focusing in long lectures.

annotations.meaningVi: thay đổi các nhiệm vụ, đa dạng hóa hoạt động
annotations.exampleEn: Good teachers vary the tasks to keep students engaged.

annotations.meaningVi: hạn chế thời gian sử dụng màn hình
annotations.exampleEn: Experts recommend limiting screen time for children under twelve.

annotations.meaningVi: tạo ra sự khác biệt đáng kể
annotations.exampleEn: Regular revision makes a significant difference to exam results.

annotations.meaningVi: đã thích nghi / yêu thích điều đó nhanh chóng
annotations.exampleEn: She took to playing the piano very well.

annotations.meaningVi: thu hút những tâm hồn trẻ tò mò
annotations.exampleEn: Science experiments are great for engaging curious young minds.

annotations.meaningVi: đề cập, chỉ ra điều cần chú ý
annotations.exampleEn: I wanted to flag a potential issue before the deadline.

annotations.meaningVi: e dè, ngần ngại không làm gì
annotations.exampleEn: She holds back from sharing her ideas in meetings.

annotations.meaningVi: lên tiếng, nói ra ý kiến của mình
annotations.exampleEn: Don't be afraid of speaking up when you disagree.
She is a thoughtful and creative problem solver.
Let's stay in touch after the conference.

grammarNotes.title: Present Perfect — Describing recent changes and experiences
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong hội thoại, thì hiện tại hoàn thành được dùng để nói về những thay đổi hoặc trải nghiệm liên quan đến hiện tại. Ví dụ: 'Tom has made some real progress', 'His essays have become much more structured', 'We've been limiting screen time'. Cấu trúc: have/has + past participle. Thì này cho thấy hành động trong quá khứ vẫn còn ảnh hưởng đến hiện tại.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The present perfect is used throughout the dialogue to describe recent changes and experiences relevant to the present moment. Examples: 'Tom has made some real progress', 'His essays have become much more structured', 'We've been limiting screen time'. Structure: have/has + past participle. This tense links past action to current relevance.

grammarNotes.title: Modal Verbs for Suggestions — might, should, would
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Giáo viên dùng các động từ khiếm khuyết để đưa ra gợi ý một cách lịch sự. 'I'd suggest practising with everyday situations' (would + suggest = đề nghị lịch sự), 'I can recommend some science books' (can = khả năng/sự sẵn sàng), 'might connect his love of reading with science' (might = khả năng xảy ra). Các modal này giúp lời đề nghị nghe nhẹ nhàng hơn là ra lệnh.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The teacher uses modal verbs to make suggestions politely. 'I'd suggest practising' (would + suggest = polite recommendation), 'I can recommend' (can = ability/willingness), 'might connect' (might = possibility). These modals soften advice, making it sound like a suggestion rather than an instruction.

translationVi:
Giáo viên: Chào buổi chiều, anh Harris. Cảm ơn anh đã đến. Mời anh ngồi.
Phụ huynh: Chào buổi chiều. Cảm ơn cô đã sắp xếp buổi gặp này. Tôi đã rất mong được nghe về tình hình của Tom.
Giáo viên: Nhìn chung, Tom đã có tiến bộ thực sự trong học kỳ này, đặc biệt là trong các bài viết. Các bài luận của em đã có cấu trúc hơn nhiều, và em rõ ràng rất yêu thích văn học.
Phụ huynh: Thật tuyệt khi nghe điều đó. Em ấy thích đọc sách ở nhà, nên điều đó có lý. Còn môn toán thì sao? Em ấy có đề cập rằng gần đây thấy khá khó.
Giáo viên: Vâng, tôi cũng nhận thấy điều đó. Em gặp khó khăn một chút với các khái niệm trừu tượng, nhưng khi chúng tôi áp dụng toán học vào các vấn đề thực tế, em tham gia tự tin hơn nhiều. Tôi nghĩ vấn đề là về sự tự tin hơn là năng lực.
Phụ huynh: Thú vị đấy. Có điều gì cụ thể tôi có thể làm ở nhà để giúp em xây dựng sự tự tin đó không?
Giáo viên: Chắc chắn rồi. Tôi gợi ý thực hành với các tình huống hàng ngày — quản lý tiền tiêu vặt, đong đo nguyên liệu khi nấu ăn, hoặc tính khoảng cách trên bản đồ. Thấy toán học trong bối cảnh thực tế thường giúp những học sinh như Tom hiểu được lý thuyết.
Phụ huynh: Đó là một gợi ý thực sự hữu ích. Tôi sẽ cố gắng cho em tham gia nhiều hơn vào những việc như vậy. Nhân tiện, giáo viên của em năm ngoái có đề cập rằng em khó tập trung trong những tiết học dài. Năm nay có gặp vấn đề đó không?
Giáo viên: Điều đó đôi khi có xảy ra. Tom có xu hướng mất tập trung sau khoảng ba mươi phút nếu hoạt động không thay đổi. Tôi đã cố gắng thay đổi các nhiệm vụ thường xuyên hơn trong lớp, điều này có vẻ giúp ích. Các nhiệm vụ ngắn và tập trung hiệu quả hơn với em so với các hoạt động dài, đơn lẻ.
Phụ huynh: Tốt để biết. Chúng tôi đã hạn chế thời gian xem màn hình ở nhà, tôi nghĩ điều đó giúp ích một chút. Em ngủ tốt hơn, và tôi nhận thấy em tỉnh táo hơn vào buổi sáng.
Giáo viên: Giấc ngủ tạo ra sự khác biệt đáng kể cho trẻ em ở độ tuổi này. Thật tuyệt khi anh đã chú ý đến điều đó. Tôi cũng khuyến khích Tom đọc sách ít nhất hai mươi phút trước khi ngủ thay vì dùng màn hình, nếu có thể.
Phụ huynh: Thực ra chúng tôi đã bắt đầu làm điều đó rồi, và em đã thích nghi tốt. Hiện tại em đang đọc một cuốn sách về vũ trụ, và em hoàn toàn bị cuốn hút.
Giáo viên: Vũ trụ là chủ đề tuyệt vời để thu hút những tâm hồn trẻ tò mò. Nếu em quan tâm, tôi có thể giới thiệu một số sách khoa học phù hợp với trình độ đọc của em, có thể kết nối niềm yêu thích đọc sách của em với môn khoa học.
Phụ huynh: Điều đó thật tuyệt, cảm ơn cô. Còn điều gì khác cô muốn đề cập trước khi chúng ta kết thúc không?
Giáo viên: Chỉ một điều — Tom đôi khi ngần ngại lên tiếng trong các cuộc thảo luận nhóm, ngay cả khi tôi biết em có những ý tưởng hay. Khuyến khích em chia sẻ ý kiến ở nhà — trong bữa ăn chẳng hạn — có thể giúp em cảm thấy thoải mái hơn khi làm điều đó trong lớp.
Phụ huynh: Tôi chắc chắn sẽ chú ý đến điều đó. Chúng tôi đôi khi ăn tối vội, nhưng tôi thấy rằng có những cuộc trò chuyện đúng nghĩa có thể rất có giá trị. Cảm ơn cô rất nhiều vì thời gian và những chia sẻ sâu sắc.
Giáo viên: Rất vui được nói chuyện. Tom là một học sinh chu đáo và sáng tạo, và với sự hỗ trợ của anh ở nhà, tôi tin tưởng em sẽ tiếp tục phát triển. Hãy giữ liên lạc nhé.

questions.prompt: In which area has Tom shown the most progress this term?
questions.explanation: The teacher says Tom has made progress 'especially in written tasks' and that 'his essays have become much more structured.'
questions.hint: The teacher mentions this in her opening summary.

questions.prompt: What does the teacher say is the real reason Tom struggles with maths?
options: He lacks the basic skills needed
He doesn't pay attention in class
It is more about confidence than ability
He finds the teacher's explanations confusing
questions.explanation: The teacher says 'I think the issue is more about confidence than ability.'
questions.hint: The teacher distinguishes between two possible causes.

questions.prompt: What practical suggestion does the teacher give for helping Tom with maths at home?
options: Use maths apps and online games
Use everyday situations like budgeting and cooking
questions.explanation: The teacher suggests 'practising with everyday situations — budgeting pocket money, measuring ingredients when cooking, or calculating distances on a map.'

questions.prompt: How long can Tom typically focus before he starts to lose concentration?
questions.explanation: The teacher says 'Tom does tend to lose focus after about thirty minutes if the activity doesn't change.'
questions.hint: The teacher gives a specific time estimate.

questions.prompt: What change has the parent made at home that has helped Tom's alertness?
options: Limiting screen time and improving sleep
questions.explanation: The parent mentions 'limiting screen time at home' and that Tom is 'sleeping better' and 'more alert in the mornings.'
questions.hint: The parent describes a change related to technology use.

questions.prompt: What does the teacher recommend Tom do before bed?
options: Read for at least twenty minutes instead of using a screen
Write in a journal about his day
questions.explanation: The teacher says 'I'd also encourage Tom to read for at least twenty minutes before bed rather than using a screen.'
questions.hint: This is the teacher's specific bedtime recommendation.

questions.prompt: What is Tom currently reading at home?
questions.explanation: The parent says 'He's currently reading a book about space, which he's absolutely fascinated by.'
questions.hint: The parent mentions the topic of Tom's current book.

questions.prompt: What social challenge does Tom face in class?
options: He argues too often with classmates
He is too shy to work in pairs
He holds back from speaking up in group discussions
questions.explanation: The teacher says 'Tom sometimes holds back from speaking up in group discussions, even when I know he has good ideas.'
questions.hint: The teacher raises this as the final point in the meeting.

questions.prompt: What does the teacher suggest to help Tom feel more comfortable speaking in class?
options: Encouraging him to share opinions at home, e.g. during meals
Putting him in a special speaking club
Asking him to read aloud every day
questions.explanation: The teacher suggests 'encouraging him to share his opinions at home — during meals, for example.'
questions.hint: The teacher proposes a home-based practice.

questions.prompt: How does the teacher describe Tom overall at the end of the meeting?
questions.explanation: The teacher says 'Tom is a thoughtful and creative student.'
questions.hint: This is the teacher's final assessment of Tom.

questions.prompt: What does the teacher offer to provide for Tom, given his interest in space?
options: Recommendations for science books at his reading level
questions.explanation: The teacher says 'I can recommend some science books at his reading level that might connect his love of reading with science as a subject.'
questions.hint: The teacher connects his reading hobby with an academic subject.

cloze.template:
Teacher: Good afternoon, Mr. Harris. Thank you for coming in. Please have a seat.
Parent: Good afternoon. Thank you for arranging this meeting. I've been looking forward to hearing how Tom is {{b1}} on.
Teacher: Overall, Tom has made some real {{b2}} this term, especially in written tasks. His essays have become much more structured, and he clearly enjoys literature.
Parent: That's great to hear. What about maths? He's mentioned finding it quite difficult lately.
Teacher: Yes, I've noticed that too. He struggles a little with abstract {{b3}}, but when we apply maths to real-world problems, he engages much more confidently. I think the issue is more about confidence than {{b4}}.
Parent: That's interesting. Is there anything specific I can do at home to help him build that confidence?
Teacher: Absolutely. I'd suggest practising with everyday situations — budgeting pocket money, measuring ingredients when cooking, or calculating distances on a map. Seeing maths in a practical context often helps students like Tom make {{b5}} of the theory.
Parent: On another note, his teacher last year mentioned he had trouble focusing during longer lessons. Has that been an issue this year?
Teacher: It has come up occasionally. Tom does tend to lose focus after about thirty minutes if the activity doesn't change. I've been trying to vary the tasks more {{b6}} in class, which seems to help.
Parent: We've been limiting screen time at home, which I think has helped a little. He's sleeping better, and I've noticed he seems more {{b7}} in the mornings.
Teacher: Sleep makes a significant difference for children at this age. I'd also encourage Tom to read for at least twenty minutes before bed rather than using a screen.
Parent: We've actually already started doing that, and he's taken to it well. He's currently reading a book about space, which he's absolutely {{b8}} by.
Teacher: Space is wonderful for engaging curious young minds. I can recommend some science books at his {{b9}} level that might connect his love of reading with science as a subject.
Parent: That would be brilliant, thank you. Is there anything else you'd like to flag?
Teacher: Tom sometimes holds back from speaking up in group discussions. Encouraging him to share his {{b10}} at home — during meals, for example — might help him feel more comfortable doing so in class.

blanks.explanation: 'Getting on' is a phrasal verb meaning to progress or manage in a particular situation.
'Made progress' is the standard collocation — 'progress' specifically means movement forward or development.
'Abstract concepts' is the phrase used in the passage to describe theoretical ideas that are difficult to visualise.
The teacher contrasts 'confidence' with 'ability' — suggesting the barrier is emotional, not intellectual.
'Make sense of' means to understand or comprehend something — a common phrasal expression.
'Vary the tasks more frequently' — 'frequently' means often; this is needed to maintain Tom's focus.
'Alert' means mentally awake and responsive — a natural result of better sleep quality.
'Absolutely fascinated by' expresses a very strong, deep interest — stronger than simply 'interested'.
'Reading level' is the specific educational term used to describe a student's reading ability.
The teacher encourages Tom to share his 'opinions' — personal views and perspectives, not just factual answers.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The teacher suggests that Tom's difficulty with maths is more about confidence than ability. To what extent do you think a student's belief in themselves affects their academic performance? Can confidence be taught, or does it only grow through success?
